elements.sent-2411-001: The large-scale structure (LSS) of the Universe, which can be measured by the clustering of galaxy and quasar tracers, provides a means to test cosmological models.
elements.sent-2411-002: Galaxy redshift surveys measure the angular coordinates and redshift distances of many galaxies and thus enable measurement of their clustering in 3D, from which cosmological information can be inferred.
elements.sent-2411-003: The Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI; \cite{Snowmass2013.Levi,DESI2016a.Science,DESI2016b.Instr,DESI2022.KP1.Instr ) is carrying out a Stage-IV redshift survey aiming to significantly improve the cosmological constraints derived from clustering measurements made with samples of galaxies, quasars and the Lyman-$\alpha$ forest.}
elements.sent-2411-004: DESI is a robotic, fibre-fed, highly multiplexed spectroscopic instrument that operates on the Nicholas U.
elements.sent-2411-005: Mayall 4-meter telescope at Kitt Peak National Observatory (KPNO) in Arizona.
elements.sent-2411-006: DESI is conducting a five-year survey over $14\,200$ square degrees, which will measure the spectra of $\sim40$ million galaxies and quasars in the redshift range $0.1<z<4.2$, covering several different target classes \cite{TS.Pipeline.Myers.2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-007: During bright time of telescope operation, DESI conducts the bright galaxy survey (BGS) at low redshifts, $0.1<z<0.4$.
elements.sent-2411-008: During dark time, DESI targets luminous red galaxies (LRGs) in the redshift range $0.4<z<1.1$, emission-line galaxies (ELGs) in $0.8<z<1.6$, and quasars (QSOs) over $0.8<z<2.1$.
elements.sent-2411-009: The Lyman-$\alpha$ forest spectral absorption in a further population of high-redshift quasars at redshifts $2.1<z<4.2$ is used to trace the distribution of neutral hydrogen, and a sample of stellar objects is also observed in the overlapping Milky Way Survey (MWS; \cite{MWS.TS.Cooper.2023 ).}
elements.sent-2411-010: During the first 13 months of main survey operation, DESI successfully observed spectra of over 18 million unique objects, more than 75\% of which are extragalactic.
elements.sent-2411-011: The key cosmological goals of clustering analyses using these data that form DESI Data Release 1 (DR1; \cite{DESI2024.I.DR1 ) include:.}
elements.sent-2411-012: These cosmological goals are achieved through measurement of the 2-point clustering signal of the different galaxy and quasar tracers.
elements.sent-2411-013: This signal is captured in the 2-point correlation function (2PCF) or its Fourier-space analogue, the power spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-014: These statistics encode the clustering of cosmological density fluctuations: however, survey operations, target selection effects, instrumental effects and astrophysical foregrounds all produce additional non-cosmological fluctuations in the observed galaxy density, and unless corrected for these contribute spurious correlations to the measured clustering.
elements.sent-2411-015: Accurately characterising the survey selection function is a key requirement for DESI.
elements.sent-2411-016: This is achieved in multiple steps, the first of which is through the creation of random catalogs of unclustered distributions of points covering the observed survey region.
elements.sent-2411-017: Then, the effects of known non-cosmological sources of density fluctuations in the data can be incorporated into this random catalog by adjusting the density or through the additional use of weights.
elements.sent-2411-018: Rather than apply weights to randoms to match data we can alternatively apply inverse weights to the data to remove effects.
elements.sent-2411-019: Our adopted approach depends on the particular nature of each effect and is detailed in this work.
elements.sent-2411-020: In the final catalogs, the ratio of weighted galaxy counts to weighted random counts is intended to produce a density field that is free from non-cosmological fluctuations.
elements.sent-2411-021: These weighted data and random catalogs are used to obtain measurements of the clustering signal that can be accurately modeled, including additional correction terms for observational effects.
elements.sent-2411-022: One purpose of this work is to identify the aspects of the analysis that require these correction terms and how they can be modeled.
elements.sent-2411-023: This paper describes the selection of the galaxy and quasar catalogs used for the cosmological analyses and released as part of DESI DR1, the creation of the random catalogs and correction of survey-specific effects and foregrounds, and measurement and validation of the 2-point clustering statistics.
elements.sent-2411-024: We summarize here the work of many supporting studies, including a technical overview of the DESI LSS catalog creation \cite{KP3s15-Ross , the pipeline for simulating DESI fiber assignment \cite{KP3s7-Lasker , the catalog blinding scheme and its validation \cite{KP3s9-Andrade,KP3s10-Chaussidon , and the creation and use of a new map of Galactic extinction based on spectra DESI has measured of stars \cite{KP3s14-Zhou .}}}}

elements.sent-2411-031: This paper is structured as follows: In \cref{sec:data , we summarize the DESI DR1 data and how it is transformed into LSS catalogs.}
elements.sent-2411-032: In \cref{sec:zcut , we describe the spectroscopic selection criteria applied to DESI DR1 LSS catalogs and present the resulting redshift distributions and sample sizes.}
elements.sent-2411-033: In \cref{sec:footprint , we present the sky geometry of the DESI DR1 LSS catalogs and the various veto masks applied within the area.}
elements.sent-2411-034: In \cref{sec:fiberassigncomp , we summarize the details of fiber assignment incompleteness in DR1 and how its effects are mitigated in both the construction and analysis of the DR1 LSS catalogs.}
elements.sent-2411-035: In \cref{sec:imsys , we present how properties of the imaging used to select DESI samples impart spurious density variation into the DR1 LSS catalogs and how we correct for this.}
elements.sent-2411-036: In \cref{sec:specsys , we summarize trends in the DESI spectroscopic success rates with DESI observing properties and how we conclude they have a negligible effect on DR1 2-point clustering measurements.}
elements.sent-2411-037: In \cref{sec:weights , we present how weights are applied to the LSS catalogs, drawing on the previous three sections, and the normalizations of the DR1 samples.}
elements.sent-2411-038: In \cref{sec:mocks , we describe how simulations of the DR1 data were produced.}
elements.sent-2411-039: In \cref{sec:clus , we present comparisons between the 2-point clustering of the DR1 data and our simulations of it.}

elements.sent-2411-045: The fibers are divided into ten `petals' and carry the light to a corresponding ten climate-controlled spectrographs.

elements.sent-2411-048: We analyze the main survey data to be released with DESI DR1 \cite{DESI2024.I.DR1 ; this includes observations through to June 14, 2022.}
elements.sent-2411-049: The DESI spectroscopic pipeline \cite{DESIpipe first processed these data the morning following observations for immediate quality checks, and then reprocessed them in a homogeneous processing run denoted as `\iron'.\footnote{It was processed with version 23.1 of the DESI software, available on NERSC via source /global/common/software/desi/desi_environment.sh 23.1.}}

elements.sent-2411-052: DESI has two distinct observing programs for large-scale structure observations, referred to as `bright' and `dark' time \cite{surveyops .}
elements.sent-2411-053: The decision on which type of tile to observe is determined prior to every exposure, depending on the observing conditions \cite{surveyops .}
elements.sent-2411-054: As described in \cite{DESItarget , dark and bright time each have their own set of target samples, with independent `Merged Target Ledgers' (MTL).}
elements.sent-2411-055: Each MTL is used to track the observation history of the targets.
elements.sent-2411-056: The states of the successfully observed targets are updated in the MTL after every tile is observed and validated so that the completed targets will no longer compete with unobserved targets for fibers.
elements.sent-2411-057: The updated MTL is then used as an input to determine which targets are assigned to what fibers on every tile, using DESI's fiberassign software \cite{FBA.Raichoor.2024 .\footnote{\url{https://github.com/desihub/fiberassign DR1 contains 2744 tiles observed in `dark' time and 2275 in `bright' time.}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Completeness, in terms of the ratio of observed spectra to total targets, is built up by overlapping tiles, nominally up to four times in bright and seven in dark time.
elements.sent-2411-059: The main survey strategy prioritizes observing tiles that overlap at any given area of the sky, after validating the quality of observations of any underlying tile \cite{surveyops , rather than covering new area.}

elements.subsection-2411-001: Target Samples used for DR1 LSS Catalogs
elements.sent-2411-061: DESI observes four classes of extra-galactic targets: quasars (QSO; \cite{QSOtarget ), luminous red galaxies (LRG; \cite{LRGtarget ), emission line galaxies (ELG; \cite{ELGtarget ), and a bright galaxy sample (BGS; \cite{BGStarget ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: All four of these classes of DESI targets were selected based on photometry from Data Release 9 (DR9) of Legacy Survey (LS) \cite{LS.Overview.Dey.2019,LS.dr9.Schegel.2024 imaging.}
elements.sent-2411-063: The LS data combines photometric data from multiple sources.
elements.sent-2411-064: DESI targeting in the North Galactic Cap (NGC) at declination $>32.375^\circ$ uses $g$ and $r$ band photometry obtained by the Beijing-Arizona Sky Survey (BASS; \cite{BASS.Zou.2017 ) and the $z$ band photometry obtained by the Mayall z-band Legacy Survey (MzLS).}
elements.sent-2411-065: At low declination and in the South Galactic Cap (SGC), all of the $g$, $r$, $z$ bands were observed using the Dark Energy Camera (DECam; \cite{DECam ), as part of the Dark Energy Camera Legacy Survey (DECaLS) and the Dark Energy Survey (DES; \cite{DES ).}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Further details on all of these imaging programs are available in \cite{LS.Overview.Dey.2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-067: These regions are denoted, respectively, as the `North' and `South' photometric regions.
elements.sent-2411-068: Infrared photometry in the $W1$ and $W2$ bands from the WISE satellite \cite{wright2010wide,lang2014unwise is used over the entire sky.}
elements.sent-2411-069: In this paper, we describe the samples used in the DESI DR1 cosmological analyses.
elements.sent-2411-070: The samples are first defined by their target bits, encoded in the DESI\_TARGET column of the target catalogs, which map directly to the priority with which targets are assigned fibers.
elements.sent-2411-071: When science targets compete for fibers, the one with the highest priority receives the assignment.
elements.sent-2411-072: Any given target can pass the selection cuts of multiple target classes and in such cases, the target is always assigned the highest of the potential priority values.
elements.sent-2411-073: We create DR1 LSS catalogs for three (nearly) distinct target samples observed exclusively in dark time (LRG, ELG, QSO) and one observed exclusively in bright time (BGS).
elements.sent-2411-074: Below, we describe the target properties of the dark time tracers in the order of greatest to least priority, then describe the bright time sample.
elements.sent-2411-075: We finally discuss the associated random samples created to enable clustering measurements.
elements.sent-2411-076: In all cases, we describe any cuts applied at the level of targeting (i.e., without any information from spectroscopic observations) that produce the samples considered for DR1 LSS catalogs.
elements.sent-2411-077: QSO are assigned the highest priority (PRIORITY value 3400 in the target catalogs).
elements.sent-2411-078: They have the lowest sky density at 310 deg$^{-2}$.
elements.sent-2411-079: They were given a high priority to ensure high completeness.
elements.sent-2411-080: This is important given the low density of the sample, which means that measurements are shot-noise-limited.
elements.sent-2411-081: Further, each target determined to have a redshift $z>2.1$ is given three additional observations at high priority (PRIORITY value 3350 in the target catalogs).
elements.sent-2411-082: These additional observations are meant to increase the signal-to-noise of spectra with Lyman-$\alpha$ forest absorption features.
elements.sent-2411-083: The full details of the QSO target selection are provided in \cite{QSOtarget .}
elements.sent-2411-084: Imaging in all of the $g$, $r$, $z$, $W1$, and $W2$ bands is used, from which a random forest algorithm selects likely quasars, restricting to data with $r<23$.
elements.sent-2411-085: This algorithm was trained and applied separately in the BASS/MzLS region, the DES region, and the DECaLS region\footnote{See the beginning of \cref{sec:target for more details on these regions.. .}}
elements.sent-2411-086: DES and DECaLS used the same instrument, but the DES region typically contains data with greater imaging depth than the DECaLS data.
elements.sent-2411-087: There are thus three distinct photometric selections for QSO applied to three distinct regions on the sky, and we correct for imaging systematics and assign redshifts to the randoms (see \cref{sec:imsys,sec:ranz ) for QSO separately in each of these three regions.}
elements.sent-2411-088: However, for the results we present, we will typically show the combined DECam (DES + DECaLS) dataset.
elements.sent-2411-089: DESI LRG targets have a sky density of just over 600 deg$^{-2}$ and are given an intermediate priority (PRIORITY value 3200 in the target catalogs).
elements.sent-2411-090: They are selected as described in \cite{LRGtarget using $g$, $r$, $z$, and $W1$ flux measurements.}
elements.sent-2411-091: The specific selection is tuned separately in the BASS/MzLS and DECam regions to obtain a sample of passively evolving galaxies with an approximately constant number density $5\times10^{-4}\;h^3$Mpc$^{-3}$ in the redshift range $0.4<z<0.8$.
elements.sent-2411-092: Above this redshift the density falls, to less than $1\times10^{-4}\;h^3$Mpc$^{-3}$ by $z=1.1$ (see \cref{fig:nzall ), due to a $z$-band fiber magnitude threshold (see \cite{LRGtarget for full details).}}
elements.sent-2411-093: The total sky density of DESI ELG targets is $\sim$ 2400 deg$^{-2}$, but the sample is somewhat complicated as it is split into three groups of different priorities.
elements.sent-2411-094: The targets are initially assigned either lower priority (`ELG\_VLO': PRIORITY value 3000, 25\% of the total) or higher priority (`ELG\_LOP': PRIORITY value 3100, 75\% of the total) based on the photometric cuts described in \cite{ELGtarget .}
elements.sent-2411-095: The same selection cuts are applied to the photometry in the BASS/MzLS and DECam regions, with a $g_{\rm fiber <24.1$ threshold.}
elements.sent-2411-096: A random selection of 10\% of both priority groups are promoted to the same priority (3200) as LRG targets, and are given an additional targeting bit `ELG\_HIP'.
elements.sent-2411-097: This boosting of priority increases the chance that pairs of LRG and ELG at small angular separations will be observed.
elements.sent-2411-098: Any ELG\_HIP is always also either VLO or LOP.
elements.sent-2411-099: For the DR1 cosmological analyses, we select only ELG\_LOP targets for the final sample, 10\% of which are also ELG\_HIP.
elements.sent-2411-100: For our analyses of DR1 data, the VLO is omitted simply due to the complexity it added to an already complicated analysis, but we plan to include it in analyses of future DESI data releases.
elements.sent-2411-101: Additionally, any of these targets that are classified as QSO by the target selection pipeline, and thus included in the QSO sample, are rejected.
elements.sent-2411-102: This removes duplicates from the DR1 analysis and simplifies the priority masking (see \cref{sec:prioritymask ).}

elements.sent-2411-160: To protect against confirmation bias in our DESI DR1 cosmological inference, we applied a blinding scheme to obscure the true cosmology during early analyses until the full large-scale structure analysis pipeline was finalised.
elements.sent-2411-161: This blinding scheme was applied at the catalog level, to produce blinded clustering catalogs for further analysis.
elements.sent-2411-162: The blinding was meant to alter three distinct pieces of information that can be extracted from the DESI 2-point measurement: 1) the location of the BAO feature; 2) the anisotropy in the clustering imparted via redshift-space distortions (RSD) due to structure growth; and 3) the large-scale scale-dependent bias that is generated by local primordial non-Gaussianity, $f_{\rm NL}$.
elements.sent-2411-163: The specific blinding method applied, and its validation using DESI simulations, is presented in \cite{KP3s9-Andrade .}
elements.sent-2411-165: The BAO and RSD signatures were blinded by shifting the measured DESI redshifts, following the methods proposed in \cite{brieden2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-166: The measured redshifts were first altered in a way that would mimic a change in the dark energy equation of state parameters $w_0$ and $w_a$.
elements.sent-2411-167: To do this, the measured true redshifts were converted to comoving distances using the DESI fiducial cosmology, and then coherently shifted based on the expected difference in redshifts between an object at that same comoving distance from an observer in a cosmological model with hidden values of $w_0$ and $w_a$, and in the fiducial cosmological model.
elements.sent-2411-168: A further shift was applied to blind the RSD structure growth measurements.
elements.sent-2411-169: To do this, RSD effects present in the measured redshifts were approximately subtracted based on an estimate of the local displacement field and the fiducial growth rate, and then new RSD shifts were applied to match the effect of a blinded growth rate value $f$ (full details of the method can be found in \cite{KP3s9-Andrade ).}
elements.sent-2411-170: The shift in $f_{\rm NL}$ was implemented by altering the weight column of the LSS catalogs, using the methodology described in \cite{KP3s10-Chaussidon .}
elements.sent-2411-171: To choose a $(w_0,w_a)$ pair for blinding, we produced a list of 1000 randomly sampled pairs, with the range of possible values bounded to keep the expected shift to the isotropic BAO scale measurement relative to its value in the fiducial cosmology to within 3\% over the redshift range $0.4<z<2.1$.
elements.sent-2411-172: The order of the pairs was randomized and they were written to a file on disk.
elements.sent-2411-173: The first time that the DESI DR1 LSS `clustering' catalogs were generated, a random integer was chosen as the row to select the $(w_0,w_a)$ pair used for DR1 blinding.
elements.sent-2411-174: The value of the integer was stored in a separate file, which was then read every subsequent time the LSS catalog production was run (following iterative improvements to the pipeline while the analysis remained blinded), so that the same blinding was consistently applied.
elements.sent-2411-175: Rather than being drawn randomly, the relative shift in $f$ was automatically calculated, using a linear RSD model as described in \cite{KP3s9-Andrade , in order to approximately compensate for the expected change due to the $w_0,w_a$ blinding in the monopole of the redshift-space clustering, but with a maximum allowed shift of up to 10\% relative to its fiducial value.}
elements.sent-2411-176: The procedure left the expected amplitude of the clustering monopole approximately unchanged by the blinding, which meant that, e.g., the clustering amplitude of the (blinded) data monopole would still be expected to match that of DESI mocks.
elements.sent-2411-177: However, the procedure imparted a shift in the amplitude of the higher-order multipoles determined by the unknown values of the $(w_0,w_a)$ pair, effectively blinding the true structure growth information in the data.
elements.sent-2411-178: The relative shift in $f_{\rm NL}$ was randomly chosen to be between $(-15,15)$, and the value applied to DR1 blinding was held fixed for all blinded catalogs by generating the value via a random seed determined from the random integer described above.
elements.sent-2411-179: The application of the blinding scheme took the `full' catalogs described in the previous subsection as inputs.
elements.sent-2411-180: These catalogs contain all of the selection function details that are described in \cref{sec:comp,sec:imsys,sec:specsys , but the blinding procedure itself changed this $n(z)$.}
elements.sent-2411-181: The completeness-corrected $n(z)$ was determined from the number density in the full catalogs and the redshifts were shifted as described above.
elements.sent-2411-182: The $n(z)$ was then re-measured and a weighting was applied to the blinded clustering catalog to make the blinded $n(z)$ match the original $n(z)$.
elements.sent-2411-183: The steps of adding radial information to the randoms and adding an `FKP' \cite{FKP weight to optimize the expected signal to noise given the number density variations then proceeded as described in \cref{sec:weights .}}
elements.sent-2411-184: Six versions of the blinded DR1 LSS catalogs were produced and their clustering measurements analysed while the LSS pipeline was iteratively improved before the first unblinded version of the catalog was created.
elements.sent-2411-185: Changes to the LSS catalogs that occurred after unblinding are described in \cref{app:catver .}

elements.sent-2411-240: In what follows, we will present the total covered area in DR1 and then step through the area removed by each type of veto mask.

elements.sent-2411-249: All veto masks associated with individual components of the DESI instrument are grouped together to define `bad hardware' regions that are to be masked from the LSS catalogs.
elements.sent-2411-250: When producing the LSS catalogs with a unique entry per target (data and randoms), we prioritize the cases that were reachable by good hardware over the cases that were not, as detailed in \cite{KP3s15-Ross .}
elements.sent-2411-251: Data and randoms are flagged as bad if they can only be reached by a fiber defined as having bad hardware in the initial compilation.
elements.sent-2411-252: This minimizes the area lost to bad hardware (as such areas are likely to be recovered as good when a tile overlaps the area on a subsequent pass) and also allows us to determine the area lost.
elements.sent-2411-253: One can see in \cref{tab:vetos that the area lost to bad hardware is only 2.3\% in bright time and 3.1\% in dark time.}
elements.sent-2411-254: Three distinct sources of information define the DR1 hardware veto:.
elements.sent-2411-255: The ZWARN\_MTL information compiled from the spectroscopic pipeline outputs (see \cref{sec:dataspec ) contains flags that indicate whether an observation passes the cuts to count as observed in the MTL.}
elements.sent-2411-256: We apply the same definition as part of the hardware mask.\footnote{A difference between what we use and what was used for MTL decisions, however, is that we are using the information as determined during the \iron\ spectroscopic reductions, and the determination for the MTL is based on the `daily' version of the spectroscopic pipeline.}
elements.sent-2411-257: We require a minimum template signal-to-noise ratio, TSNR2.
elements.sent-2411-258: These values are determined for each tracer type and are proportional to the effective observing time.
elements.sent-2411-259: They are determined per coadded spectrum, but are independent of the target observed to produce the spectrum; they use a fixed template and the estimated noise.
elements.sent-2411-261: In dark time, we apply a threshold $TSNR2\_ELG > 80$ for all samples, while in bright time, we apply a threshold $TSNR2\_BGS > 1000$.
elements.sent-2411-262: The spectroscopic success rates for spectra with TSNR2 values below these thresholds decline dramatically and these cuts remove less than 1\% of the observed data.
elements.sent-2411-263: All tile and fiber combinations below these thresholds are marked as bad hardware.
elements.sent-2411-264: We identify 60 poor-performing fibers, as defined in \cite{KP3s3-Krolewski .}
elements.sent-2411-265: All data from those fibers are flagged as bad hardware.

elements.sent-2411-267: To account for the areas on the sky where a given target type could not be observed, we apply a priority veto mask.
elements.sent-2411-268: Similar to the hardware veto, the priority veto is applied to the initial compilation of reachable data and random targets and is based on the metadata associated with the potential assignments; i.e., it is determined purely from the fiber assignment information.
elements.sent-2411-269: For both data and randoms, before cutting to unique objects, the priority of every target assigned on the given tile and fiber is known and stored in the catalogs as the column PRIORITY\_ASSIGNED.
elements.sent-2411-270: If the PRIORITY\_ASSIGNED is greater than that of the sample under consideration, the object's particular occurrence (associated with the given tile and fiber) is vetoed.
elements.sent-2411-271: Note that the given object can still be included (i.e., not ultimately vetoed) in the final catalog if it was a potential assignment on a different tile or fiber.
elements.sent-2411-272: For our dark time DESI DR1 samples, only QSO and rare high-priority strong lens candidates with priority 4000 cause priority vetoes.
elements.sent-2411-273: We choose not to have LRG targets cause priority vetos on ELGs, as 10\% of ELGs have the same priority as LRGs and there is significant overlap between the samples in redshift.
elements.sent-2411-274: For BGS, only white dwarf candidates have a higher priority (2998) than our BGS sample and cause a priority veto.
elements.sent-2411-275: The priority mask for the LRG and ELG samples is caused by the same QSO and strong lens candidates and it is thus the same area of 1666.7 deg$^{2}$ for both, which is 20\% of the DR1 footprint.

elements.sent-2411-555: For the corrections we ultimately apply to the DR1 LSS catalogs, for all tracers we model the success rate, $G_z$, as a function of the template signal-to-noise ratio, TSNR2.
elements.sent-2411-556: These TSNR2 values are determined for each tracer type and per coadded spectrum, but are independent of the specific properties of the target (e.g., its brightness) observed to produce the spectrum; they use a fixed template (different for each tracer type) for the signal and the estimated noise and the result is thus proportional to an estimated effective observing time.

elements.sent-2411-558: For the BGS, LRG, and QSO samples, we use the fiberflux of the observed target as a secondary variable in the modeling \cite{KP3s3-Krolewski .}
elements.sent-2411-559: For ELGs, $G_z$ depends strongly on the precise redshift of the galaxy, due to noise from sky lines interfering with the ability to detect the [OII] doublet.
elements.sent-2411-560: The success rate is thus modeled as a function of TSNR2\_ELG and the galaxy redshift, as described in \cite{KP3s4-Yu .}
elements.sent-2411-561: In all cases, to apply weights we find the relative success at the given redshift or FIBERFLUX, $\phi_{\rm fib}$.
elements.sent-2411-562: For BGS, LRG, and QSO, the success model is asymptotic and the weight can thus be defined as \begin{equation}w_{\rm zfail}(S_{\rm ratio},\phi_{\rm fib}) = G_z (\infty,\phi_{\rm fib})/G_z( S_{\rm ratio},\phi_{\rm fib}), \label{eqn:wzfail}\end{equation} where $S_{\rm ratio}$ is the TSNR2 value and $G_z$ is a function modelling the success rate as a function of FIBERFLUX and TSNR2.
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}w_{\rm zfail}(S_{\rm ratio},\phi_{\rm fib}) = G_z (\infty,\phi_{\rm fib})/G_z( S_{\rm ratio},\phi_{\rm fib}), \label{eqn:wzfail}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-563: As TSNR2 goes to infinity, $G_z$ must asymptote to a constant value at fixed FIBERFLUX; we use an error function to achieve this behavior, fit it to the observed redshift failure rates, and evaluate it in the limit of $TSNR2 \rightarrow \infty$ to determine the numberator of Eq.~\ref{eqn:wzfail .}
elements.sent-2411-564: For the ELGs, a linear relationship is determined for the expected success rate as a function of TSNR2\_ELG at each redshift.
elements.sent-2411-565: These linear relationships are normalized to have a mean of one, weighting by the effective observing time.
elements.sent-2411-566: For all tracers, the modeling approach described above means that we do not over-correct for samples that have an overall poor spectroscopic success rate (i.e., when $G_z( (\infty,\phi_{\rm fib )) $ is much less than 1); e.g., in the most extreme case, QSO at low fiberflux have a $\sim30\%$ success rate and rather than upweight them all by a factor of $\sim$ 3, we only correct for the $\sim$ 10\% variation in the success rate of the subsample as a function of effective observing time.}

elements.subsection-2411-016: Accuracy and Precision of Redshift Estimation
elements.sent-2411-568: Uncertainties in the estimation of the DESI redshift impact the measured clustering, but are not accounted for in the LSS catalogs.
elements.sent-2411-569: These uncertainties include the random scatter from standard measurement noise, systematic biases in the redshifts, and also catastrophic errors that are due to, e.g., mis-identification of emission lines or confusion with sky lines.
elements.sent-2411-570: For LRG, redshift accuracy can be assessed using repeated observations and is estimated at a standard deviation of 40--60 km s$^{-1}$ \cite{Yu23,VIGalaxies.Lan.2023 (note that different statistics are used, with \cite{Yu23 quoting the standard deviation and \cite{VIGalaxies.Lan.2023 quoting the normalized median absolute deviation).}}}
elements.sent-2411-571: Its dependence on magnitude is measured in \cite{VIGalaxies.Lan.2023 and on redshift in \cite{Yu23 , who show that the distribution of redshift errors is best fit by a Lorentzian distribution.}}
elements.sent-2411-572: Comparison to BOSS redshifts in \cref{sec:compare_to_sdss shows that only 0.3\% of the LRG targets in common differ by more than 0.002 in redshift ($\sim$250 km s$^{-1}$ at typical LRG redshifts), with a mean difference of 4 km s$^{-1}$ (very similar to the mean difference of 3.3 km s$^{-1}$ found in \cite{VIGalaxies.Lan.2023 from comparison to DEEP2).}}
elements.sent-2411-573: Since BGS is brighter than LRG, its redshifts are more accurate: the standard deviation is 10 km s$^{-1}$ with little dependence on $r_{fiber}$ \cite{VIGalaxies.Lan.2023 , with a mean difference of 6.5 km s$^{-1}$ compared to DEEP2 or 2 km s$^{-1}$ compared to BOSS, and a 0.2\% outlier fraction.}

elements.sent-2411-764: In what follows, we detail the general steps that are applied to all mocks to turn them into mock DESI DR1 LSS catalogs.

elements.sent-2411-784: For DESI DR1, we have developed and applied three separate treatments to account for DESI fiber assignment in the mock catalogs, which we label as mock ``flavors''.

elements.sent-2411-786: We are thus able to utilize the same random angular coordinates as used for the real data full\_noveto samples (see \cref{sec:LSScat ), and with the only difference in the veto masks being the realization specific priority veto (see \cref{sec:prioritymask ).}}

elements.sent-2411-788: The only variation concerns the priority veto mask, which we describe below.

elements.sent-2411-792: We expect all completeness statistics to match those of the DESI data up to the level that the underlying simulations match the DR1 data target samples\footnote{One example of the simulations being imperfect is they are not full lightcones and thus, e.g., the `redshift' at which QSO and ELG targets overlap do not correspond to the redshift output of the simulations.. .}
elements.sent-2411-793: Therefore we developed a ``fast-fiberassign'' (FFA) \cite{KP3s6-Bianchi method to simulate DESI fiber assignment.}
elements.sent-2411-794: The method uses a shallow learning algorithm to produce a fiber assignment emulator that takes the local angular density, determined via friends-of-friends with a linking length that is tuned per tracer, and $n_{\rm tile}$ as input variables.
elements.sent-2411-795: The training and assignment are applied individually to each tracer type.
elements.sent-2411-796: This means that the priority veto mask does not get applied; instead, the average effect of, e.g., QSO on LRG targets as a function of $n_{\rm tile}$ is learned, and then the FFA assignments are based on the local density of the given tracer type and $n_{\rm tile}$.

elements.sent-2411-896: We have presented the details of LSS catalogs obtained from the DESI DR1 data, and their validation for use in DESI 2024 cosmological analyses.
elements.sent-2411-897: The catalogs contain over 5.7 million unique tracers and will be made publicly available with DESI DR1.
elements.sent-2411-898: We have presented the methodology for the measurement of the multipoles of 2-point functions used in DESI 2024 cosmological analyses in \cref{sec:2pt .}
elements.sent-2411-899: The raw clustering measurements obtained from these LSS catalogs are biased compared to naive theoretical expectations, due to a combination of effects from fiber assignment incompleteness, survey geometry, and integral constraint effects.
elements.sent-2411-900: Where necessary, corrections for these effects, through the combination of the application of window functions to models and correction terms to the 2-point clustering are detailed in \cref{sec:2pt .. Specifically:.}
elements.sent-2411-901: The raw clustering measurements of post-reconstruction configuration-space 2-point functions were demonstrated to provide unbiased BAO measurements \cite{DESI2024.III.KP4 .}
elements.sent-2411-902: Fiber assignment incompleteness biases both the total and relative amplitudes of the measured 2-point multipoles.
elements.sent-2411-903: When fitting the full-shape of DESI 2-point functions in \cite{DESI2024.III.KP4 , this effect is mitigated by the removal of clustering information from angular scales less than 0.05 degrees, directly in the estimator for the measurements and in the window function for the model, with full details presented in \cite{KP3s5-Pinon .}}
elements.sent-2411-904: The survey geometry is accounted for using a standard window function application in Fourier-space.
elements.sent-2411-905: The LSS catalog randoms sample the data to obtain radial information.
elements.sent-2411-906: This induces a radial integral constraint (RIC) and nulls purely radial modes in the clustering measurements.
elements.sent-2411-907: The corrections applied for imaging systematics induce stronger angular integral constraints (AIC) than would otherwise be present.
elements.sent-2411-908: We describe how an empirical correction based on the results of simulations is applied to the measured power spectra used for cosmological constraints in companion papers.
elements.sent-2411-909: Any clustering analyses that use DESI DR1 LSS catalogs may have to derive their own corrections, specific to their own clustering measurements.

elements.sent-2411-922: The 2-point clustering of DESI DR1 data in configuration- and Fourier-space is generally consistent with that of simulations of DESI DR1, within a 2\% factor of galaxy bias.
elements.sent-2411-923: In Fourier-space, reducing the scale range to $k<0.2h$Mpc$^{-1}$ is necessary for reasonable agreement for BGS, LRG $0.8<z<1.1$, and QSO.

elements.sent-2411-926: The catalogs we have presented are intended for cosmological analysis of 2-point functions on large-scales ($s\gtrsim 20 h^{-1}$Mpc, $k\lesssim 0.2 h$Mpc$^{-1}$).
elements.sent-2411-927: For higher-order and alternative clustering measurements that use similarly large scales that are significantly greater than those affected by fiber collisions ($\sim$0.05 degrees), we recommend using the same catalogs and using the information outlined above to account for any biases and/or systematic uncertainties in the measurements.

elements.sent-2411-930: Despite the issues described above, observational systematic uncertainties are sub-dominant to the statistical uncertainties in the DESI 2024 cosmological analyses \cite{DESI2024.III.KP4,DESI2024.V.KP5 .}

elements.sent-2411-974: The LSS catalogs that were first used for unblinded DR1 BAO measurements are version `v1'.
elements.sent-2411-975: Afterward, small bugs were found and fixed that produced subsequent versions of the LSS catalogs.
elements.sent-2411-976: The first was that initially, groups of overlapping tiles found in the randoms but not the data were given zero completeness for the parameter $f_{\rm tile}$, defined in \cref{sec:compdef .}
elements.sent-2411-977: Such groups of tiles tend to be small areas with a large number of overlapping tiles, which would thus likely have a completeness of 1 if any targets existed in the area.
elements.sent-2411-978: Versions `v1.2' and higher have $f_{\rm tile}$ set to 1 in these regions.
elements.sent-2411-979: The effect was greatest on the QSO sample, as it is the most sparse and thus the most likely to have targets absent from any group of overlapping tiles.
elements.sent-2411-980: After fixing the issue, we confirmed that the large-scale clustering of the (blinded) QSO sample was consistent whether we applied $f_{\rm tile}$ to the randoms or 1/$f_{\rm tile}$ to the data.
elements.sent-2411-981: In `v1', before the fix, the large-scale power of the QSO sample is significantly higher for the (fiducial) case where we apply $f_{\rm tile}$ to the randoms.
elements.sent-2411-982: Version v1.2 of the LSS catalogs for the DESI cosmology results in \cite{DESI2024.III.KP4,DESI2024.VI.KP7A .}
elements.sent-2411-983: A second issue was discovered where the `template signal-to-noise ratio' TSNR2 threshold cut (defined in \cref{sec:hardware_veto ) was applied only when making the clustering catalogs.}
elements.sent-2411-984: This was not a logically consistent choice, as applying such a threshold is equivalent to including the threshold in the `good hardware' definition (and is part of this definition in the final catalogs).
elements.sent-2411-985: This logical inconsistency propagated to small differences in the angular distribution of data and randoms.
elements.sent-2411-986: It falsely increased the determination of the number of overlapping tiles (as all good hardware instances contribute to the number of overlapping tiles).
elements.sent-2411-987: This was fixed in version v1.3 (and all subsequent versions) by applying the TSNR2 threshold as part of the good hardware definition.
elements.sent-2411-988: Before any Full-Shape analyses were unblinded, one further issue was found.
elements.sent-2411-989: A correlation was induced between different subsets of randoms due to the same random seed being used for the assignment of radial information to the random catalogs.
elements.sent-2411-990: This was fixed by explicitly setting a different random seed.
elements.sent-2411-991: Therefore, a version v1.4 was created, using a new stream-lined pipeline script.
elements.sent-2411-992: After v1.4 was frozen, it was noticed that the updated pipeline script had not included the application of the custom mask for the ELG sample (but it had been applied to v1.2).
elements.sent-2411-993: Thus, a v1.5 was created, which is used for the Full-Shape analysis \cite{DESI2024.V.KP5 .}
elements.sent-2411-994: We publicly release both versions v1.2 and v1.5.
elements.section-2411-017: Catalogs for small-scale clustering measurements (PIP weighted)
elements.sent-2411-995: As discussed in \cref{sec:comp , the fiducial approach to mitigating fiber assignment incompleteness in the DESI 2024 cosmological analysis is to remove the angular scales within a DESI fiber patrol radius, $\theta <0.05$ degrees \cite{KP3s5-Pinon .}}
elements.sent-2411-996: The fiducial DR1 LSS catalogs described throughout are built to be optimal for this application.
elements.sent-2411-997: Alternatively, one can correct for the effects of fiber assignment incompleteness at any scale via the combination of PIP weights and angular upweights \cite{2017Bianchi .}
elements.sent-2411-998: The LSS catalogs that support such measurements must be constructed differently and are released as v1.5pip.
elements.sent-2411-999: We detail what is different about them below.
elements.sent-2411-1000: As described in \cref{sec:altmtl , we obtain a total of 129 realizations of the fiber assignment of the DESI DR1 data; one for the real data and 128 using the altmtl method described in \cite{KP3s7-Lasker .}}
elements.sent-2411-1001: Each target in the full catalogs has information on whether it was assigned or not in each of the 128 altmtl realizations stored in a bit array, $w^{(b)}$ and the total probability of assignment stored as $p_{\rm obs = (1 + c) / (1 + 128)$, with $c=\mathrm{popcnt (w^{(b) )$ the number of 1 bits in $w^{(b)}$, i.e. the number of realisations in which the target is assigned.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1002: Using these data, the completeness weights for the data, $w_{\rm comp}$ are determined as 1/$p_{\rm obs}$; this is the individual inverse probability (IIP).
elements.sent-2411-1003: The process outlined in \cref{sec:FKP is then followed to obtain $w_{\rm tot}$ and $w_{\rm FKP}$, except the weights for the randoms are not multiplied by $f_{\rm tile}$, as the IIP weights account for any source of fiber assignment incompleteness.}
elements.sent-2411-1004: The process described above provides the LSS catalogs with the needed data to measure PIP-weighted 2-point clustering measurements.
elements.sent-2411-1005: Then, each pair weight, $w^{PIP _{ij}$ is obtained as \begin{equation}\label{eq:w_pip} w^{PIP}_{ij} = w_{\rm comp,i} w_{\rm comp,j} \frac{w^{\rm eff}_{ij}}{g_{\rm 128}(c_i,c_j)} \ ,\end{equation} where $g_{\rm 128}$ (defined fully in \cite{KP3s6-Bianchi ) represents the expectation value of $w^{PIP}$ in the limit of independent probabilities and $w^{\rm eff _{ij = (1 + 128) / (1 + \mathrm{popcnt (w_{i ^{(b) \& w_{j ^{(b) ))$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-022: \begin{equation}\label{eq:w_pip} w^{PIP}_{ij} = w_{\rm comp,i} w_{\rm comp,j} \frac{w^{\rm eff}_{ij}}{g_{\rm 128}(c_i,c_j)} \ ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1006: As described in detail in \cite{KP3s6-Bianchi , this expression is more robust to scale-dependent artifacts induced by the combination of low probabilities and low number of realisations of the targeting.}
elements.sent-2411-1007: The randoms are still weighted by $w_{\rm tot}$.
elements.figure-2411-029: \includegraphics[width=0.49\columnwidth]{figures/ELG1_wpcomp.png} \includegraphics[width=0.49\columnwidth]{figures/ELG1_Y1SV3errcomp.png} \caption{For ELG data with $0.8<z<1.1$, the left-hand plot displays the projected clustering when using PIP and angular up-weighting comparing the results from EDR and DR1 data (open vs. filled symbols). The EDR points have their $r_p$ values slightly offset for legibility. We also compare to the results obtained using the default DR1 weights, which are known to not properly correct the measurements on small scales. The right-hand plot compares uncertainty recovered from 128 jack-knife realizations of the same DR1/EDR PIP and angular up-weighted projected clustering measurements. } \label{fig:ELGwppipcomp}
elements.sent-2411-1008: For DESI DR1 LSS catalogs, angular up-weighting is additionally necessary.
elements.sent-2411-1009: This is due to much of the area being covered by only 1 or 2 tiles, which leads to a large number of pairs that have 0 probability and thus will not be included in the PIP weights.
elements.sent-2411-1010: The angular up-weighting uses the angular separation of pairs in the `full' LSS catalog, using all of the data (`parent' in \cite{2017Bianchi ), and also the selection of the data that was assigned a fiber and observed (`fibered' in \cite{2017Bianchi ).}}
elements.sent-2411-1011: To do so consistently, we must include weights that account for the variation of $w_{\rm tot /w_{\rm comp}$ and $w_{\rm FKP}$ with $n_{\rm tile}$.}
elements.sent-2411-1012: The mean value of these quantities in the data catalog is determined as a function of $n_{\rm tile}$ and is added as two new columns to the full LSS catalog.\footnote{They are WEIGHT\_NTILE and WEIGHT\_FKP\_NTILE.}
elements.sent-2411-1013: These weights are then applied when obtaining the pair counts used for the angular up-weighting.
elements.sent-2411-1014: In practice, angular up-weights $DD_{\mathrm{parent (\theta) / DD_{\mathrm{fibered (\theta)$ and $DR_{\mathrm{parent (\theta) / DR_{\mathrm{fibered (\theta)$ are pre-computed as a function of angular separation $\theta$ with a logarithmic binning (40 bins from $10^{-4}$ to $10^{0.5 \; \mathrm{deg}$).}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1015: When running pair counts with angular up-weights, these weights are interpolated linearly at the cosine separation $\cos{\theta}$ between the galaxies of each pair.
elements.sent-2411-1016: To illustrate the impact of PIP and angular up-weighting on the measured clustering signal, we present the measured projected clustering applying PIP and angular up-weights to the DR1 v1.5pip catalogs for ELGs with $0.8<z<1.1$ in \cref{fig:ELGwppipcomp .}
elements.sent-2411-1017: We compare the results to those obtained from the same weighting of EDR (SV3) \cite{abacusHODELG and those obtained from the default catalogs (v1.5) and default weighting.}
elements.sent-2411-1018: The projected clustering $w_p(r_p)$ is defined by \begin{equation}w_p(r_p) = \int_{-r_{\pi,{\rm max}}}^{r_{\pi,{\rm max}}} \xi(r_p,r_\pi){\rm d}r_\pi,\end{equation} where $r_{\pi}$ is the line of sight separation $s\mu$ (with $s$ and $\mu$ defined in \cref{sec:xicalc ) and $r_p$ is the projected separation $\sqrt{1-\mu^2 s$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1019: One can observe that the PIP and angular up-weights are required to obtain small-scale clustering that is in rough agreement with the SV3 results.
elements.sent-2411-1020: The sky area covered in SV3 was covered up to 13 times to minimize fiber assignment incompleteness, making it ideal for measuring small-scale clustering without major systematic concerns stemming from fiber assignment.
elements.sent-2411-1021: The SV3 clustering at large scales is systematically greater than the DR1 clustering, which is likely due to some combination of correlated noise, uncorrected imaging systematics affecting the SV3 measurements, and variations in the intrinsic DESI ELG galaxy population with sky location.
elements.sent-2411-1022: In the right-hand panel, we compare the uncertainty obtained from 128 jackknife realizations of the DR1 and SV3 clustering.
elements.sent-2411-1023: One can observe that the uncertainty at small scales is similar for DR1 and SV3, but the improvement on large-scales is dramatic for DR1 compared to SV3.
elements.sent-2411-1024: As DESI improves its coverage so that median number of overlapping tiles is greater than 4, the small-scale uncertainty will be dramatically improved.

The right-hand plot compares the same cases, but for the uncertainty determined from 60 jack-knife samples.} \label{fig:ELGpipcomp}

elements.sent-2411-1026: Based on comparisons of jackknife error estimates, we find that they are noisier on large scales than the results from the fiducial catalogs (v1.5).

elements.sent-2411-1028: In that figure, we compare the results obtained from the v1.5 catalogs with our default weighting ($w_{\rm tot w_{\rm FKP}$ on both data and randoms) to that obtained from v1.5pip with the same weighting.}
elements.sent-2411-1029: The difference is therefore that the IIP weight is used for v1.5pip $w_{\rm comp}$ and there is no $f_{\rm tile}$ weighting applied to the randoms.

elements.sent-2411-1035: For the DESI 2024 cosmological analyses, we consistently applied a common color scheme associated with each tracer and redshift bin.
elements.sent-2411-1036: This enabled easy comparisons of the results.
elements.sent-2411-1037: Here, we describe the color scheme and some of the motivation behind the choices.
elements.sent-2411-1038: Certain choices were obvious: LRGs are red and ELGs are blue.
elements.sent-2411-1039: The QSO sample was given green because SDSS \cite{ebosslss did so for a reason not remembered; the particular shade\footnote{All colors in `' refer to named colors from \url{https://matplotlib.org/stable/gallery/color/named_colors.html .}}}
elements.sent-2411-1040: The Lyman $\alpha$ forest is observed at the lowest wavelengths of DESI spectra, so they were awarded purple.
elements.sent-2411-1041: The LRG redshift bins were given the progression orange, `orangered', and `firebrick'.
elements.sent-2411-1042: For the ELGs, we used `skyblue' and `steelblue' for $0.8<z<1.1$ and $1.1<z<1.6$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-1043: They make a handsome and distinguishable pair.
elements.sent-2411-1044: The combination of LRG and ELG in the $0.8<z<1.1$ redshift range was given the color `slateblue', which is a quite lovely shade of purple that is distinguishable from any color used by the Lyman-$\alpha$ forest group.
elements.sent-2411-1045: For BGS, chose `yellowgreen' to make sure it was especially distinguishable from the lowest LRG bin.
